title: The Fyodorov-Hiary-Keating conjecture
speaker: Paul Bourgade
abstract: Through the random matrix analogy, Fyodorov, Hiary and Keating conjectured very precisely the typical values of the Riemann zeta function in short intervals of the critical line, in particular their maximum. Their prediction relied on techniques from statistical mechanics such as the freezing scenario. Recent rigorous progress has exploited underlying branching structures instead, both for random characteristic polynomials and L-functions. I will explain these developments, in particular joint work with Arguin and Radziwill.

proof_stages.label: Randomized FHK maximum problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the randomized height model for the maximum of |zeta(1/2+i(tau+h))| on a unit window and states the Fyodorov-Hiary-Keating prediction with its 3/4 logarithmic correction and universal right tail.

proof_stages.label: Log-correlated universality and model evidence
proof_stages.summary: The zeta maximum is placed in the general framework of log-correlated fields, where random Fourier series, circular logREM, branching Brownian motion, and the two-dimensional GFF explain the universality of the 3/4 shift, freezing heuristics, and barrier methods.

proof_stages.label: Random-matrix route and zeta theorem
proof_stages.summary: The FHK conjecture for zeta is traced through the corresponding CUE characteristic-polynomial conjecture and moment-of-moments calculations, after which the speaker states the available zeta results: tightness after centering and uniform y exp(-2y) right-tail bounds.

proof_stages.label: Selberg-prime branching heuristics and obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Selberg's central limit theorem and short prime sums suggest a branching random-walk decomposition for log zeta, but high moments of prime sums fail at the ballistic scale needed for tight extremes, creating the main number-theoretic barrier.

proof_stages.label: Multiscale corridor and lower-barrier induction
proof_stages.summary: The proof is organized as an induction over iterated-logarithmic scales, showing that any high endpoint must remain inside an upper and lower corridor, with the lower barrier preventing the need to estimate inaccessible large deviations.

proof_stages.label: Twisted moments and Dirichlet-polynomial encoding
proof_stages.summary: The analytic input is a tailored twisted/mollified moment estimate using truncated Mobius-type Dirichlet polynomials and low-prime-multiplicity restrictions to decouple prime scales and encode the barrier events.

proof_stages.label: Consequences, extensions, and global-maximum heuristics
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by interpreting the result as evidence for universal log-correlated extreme statistics, discussing limitations for other L-functions and GMC convergence, and comparing FHK window tails with speculative global-maximum conjectures.

examples.name: Random Fourier wave on the circle
examples.note: The Gaussian Fourier series with coefficients scaled by 1/sqrt(k) makes the log-correlated covariance concrete by showing how equal variance contributions from all frequency scales lead to logarithmic correlations.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes down the random Fourier-wave construction with Gaussian modes and explains the 1/sqrt(k) normalization by checking that exponential frequency blocks have comparable variance

examples.name: CUE characteristic-polynomial maximum
examples.note: The maximum of the logarithm of the characteristic polynomial of a random unitary matrix is the random-matrix analogue from which the FHK prediction for zeta is motivated via moment-of-moments calculations.
examples.engagement_reason: the CUE characteristic-polynomial maximum is cited via conjectures, known results, numerics, and analogy with Fisher-Hartwig/moment calculations, but no concrete calculation is carried out on it in the transcript

examples.name: Fyodorov-Bouchaud circular logREM
examples.note: The exactly Gaussian circular logarithmic random energy model is used to display the moment/freezing calculation that produced the predicted 3/4 logarithmic shift and limiting extreme-value density.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker defines the circular logREM covariance, discusses its maximum asymptotics, then works through the partition-function/moment and freezing-scenario heuristics leading to the predicted law

examples.name: Branching Brownian motion and FKPP
examples.note: Branching Brownian motion illustrates the origin of the 3/4 correction and, through Bramson's barrier method, supplies the conceptual prototype for the later multiscale barrier argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs branching Brownian motion, relates its maximum to FKPP, compares the independent heuristic with Bramson’s correction, and explains the barrier/second-moment and Ballot-theorem calculation

examples.name: Naive independent-Gaussian sampling model
examples.note: Treating values in the zeta window as independent Gaussians gives the correct leading scale but the wrong logarithmic correction, thereby showing why log-correlations must be incorporated.
examples.engagement_reason: the independent-Gaussian model is used to compute heuristic maxima, giving the leading order and the incorrect -1/4 logarithmic correction in both BBM and zeta contexts

passes.reason: The iid Gaussian comparison collapses the correlation structure and gives the wrong logarithmic correction, serving as the foil to the log-correlated prediction.

examples.name: Zeta plus a Dirichlet L-function
examples.note: In response to an audience question, a linear combination of the Riemann zeta function and a Dirichlet L-function with the same gamma factor is used to illustrate the expected robustness of value statistics beyond settings with an Euler product or RH-like zero behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: in the discussion they reason through the linear combination zeta plus a Dirichlet L-function, using independence and alternating dominance to infer why its value statistics should match those of the individual functions
